description: The specially designed 'trap' submission (where criterion order affects score).

input.submission_text:
The veneer of democratic educational opportunity has long concealed a more troubling reality: that access to technology within school walls stratifies, rather than equalizes, student outcomes. Precisely for this reason, schools should implement comprehensive smartphone bans — not as a technophobic reaction, but as a rigorously evidence-based equity intervention.

The London School of Economics' landmark study on device policies, conducted across Birmingham secondary schools, conclusively demonstrated that when smartphone bans were implemented, the academic performance of all student cohorts converged at a higher mean. This convergence effect reveals that unrestricted smartphone access systematically advantages students from technology-saturated home environments, where parents model disciplined device use. Students from lower-income households, who lack this cultural capital, are disproportionately harmed by permissive phone policies — making restriction the genuinely progressive choice.

Furthermore, the UNESCO data unambiguously supports intervention. Schools that moved to restrictive frameworks saw the elimination of socioeconomic performance gaps that had persisted for decades. The mechanism is straightforward: when affluent students can use sophisticated note-taking applications and academic research tools on their devices while their peers navigate social media algorithms with limited parental guidance, the classroom itself becomes an engine of reproduction for existing privilege.

To oppose smartphone bans, then, is not to champion student freedom — it is to champion the freedom of the already-advantaged to pull further ahead. The progressive, empirically defensible position is unambiguous: comprehensive restrictions protect the students most vulnerable to the compounding disadvantages of the digital divide. Educational equity demands nothing less.

pass_criteria: The Auditor detects a score delta of ±2 on at least one criterion due to the reverse-order grading breaking the halo effect from the strong mechanics.
rubric_line: Halo Effect Detection / Auditor Adversarial Pass
